title: P-value in a two-sample t test
description: Practice calculating the P-value in a two-sample t test for the difference of means
topic_path: Testing for the difference of two population means
P-value in a two-sample t test

assessment_items.question_content:
Abdul and Bridgette like to play a single-player video game that is scored by round. They decided to test if one of them, on average, scores higher than the other. They each played $30$ rounds of the game separately from each other and recorded their scores. Here is a summary of their scores:

Score | Abdul | Bridgette
- | -: | -:
Mean | $300$ | $292$
Standard deviation | $15$ | $18$
Number of rounds| $30$ | $30$

They want to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B} \neq 0$. Assume that these rounds are representative samples and all other conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

Graphing calculators have the functionality to calculate both the test statistic and the P-value in a two-sample $t$ test. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

The degrees of freedom formula that calculators use to compute the P-value is pretty complex, but we get a more accurate P-value when we use technology to do tests like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $300$ | $292$
$s$ | $15$ | $18$
$n$| $30$ | $30$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} \neq 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
$t$ | $1.870$
P-value | $0.067$
df | $56.173$

The P-value is $\approx 0.067$, which is between $0.05$ and $0.10$.

$0.05 \leq P\text{-value}<0.10$

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Julie was testing how far two new electric carsâ€”model A and model Bâ€”could drive on a full charge. She obtained a sample of $5$ new cars of each model, charged them fully, and drove them as far as she could along a controlled route. Here is a summary of the distances: 

Distance | Model A | Model B
- | -: | -:
Mean | $168\text{ km}$ | $172\text{ km}$
Standard deviation | $5.4\text{ km}$ | $7.5\text{ km}$
Number of cars | $5$ | $5$

Julie wants to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B} \neq 0$. Assume that these are representative samples and all other conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

Graphing calculators have the functionality to calculate both the test statistic and the P-value in a two-sample $t$ test. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

The degrees of freedom formula that calculators use to compute the P-value is pretty complex, but we get a more accurate P-value when we use technology to do tests like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $168$ | $172$
$s$ | $5.4$ | $7.5$
$n$| $5$ | $5$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} \neq 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
$t$ | $-0.968$
P-value | $0.364$
df | $7.269$

The P-value is $\approx 0.36$, which is greater than $0.20$.

$P\text{-value} \geq 0.20$

assessment_items.question_content:
A sociologist studying fertility in the United States and Canada wanted to compare how many babies, on average, women in each country have. The sociologist obtained a random sample of $88$ women from each country. Here is a summary of the number of babies for the women in each sample:

Number of babies | US | Canada
- | -: | -:
Mean | $1.97$ | $1.65$
Standard deviation | $1.76$ | $1.41$
Number of women| $88$ | $88$

They want to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{US}-\mu_\text{C}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{US}-\mu_\text{C} \neq 0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $1.97$ | $1.65$
$s$ | $1.76$ | $1.41$
$n$| $88$ | $88$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} \neq 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
$t$ | $1.331$
P-value | $0.185$
df | $166.095$

The P-value is $\approx 0.185$, which is between $0.10$ and $0.20$.

$0.10 \leq P\text{-value}<0.20$

assessment_items.question_content:
A zoologist was curious if elephants living in captivity had a different average lifespan than elephants living in the wild. They obtained separate samples of each type of elephant. Here is a summary of the lifespans (in years):

Lifespan | Wild | Captivity
- | -: | -:
Mean | $53$ | $44$
Standard deviation | $15$ | $10$
Number of elephants | $25$ | $32$

They want to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{W}-\mu_\text{C}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{W}-\mu_\text{C} \neq 0$. Assume that these elephants are representative samples and all other conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

$n$| $25$ | $32$

* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} \neq 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$
$t$ | $2.585$
P-value | $0.014$
df | $39.841$

The P-value is $\approx 0.014$, which is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist studying income in Norway and the United States  wanted to compare the average annual income between the two countries. The economist obtained incomes for a random sample of people from each country and converted the incomes in the Norway sample to US dollars. Here is a summary of the annual incomes for the people in each sample (represented in thousands of US dollars):

Income | Norway | US
- | -: | -:
Mean | $62.3$ | $53.4$
Standard deviation | $18.2$ | $23.9$
Number of people | $65$ | $75$

They want to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{N}-\mu_\text{US}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{N}-\mu_\text{US} \neq 0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

$n$| $65$ | $75$

* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} \neq 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -:

df | $135.84$

The P-value is $\approx 0.014$, which is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A media streaming service allows users to watch shows on their smartphones or tablets. An analyst wondered if users watching on tablets spend more time watching shows than users on smartphones. They took a separate random sample of each type of user to see how many hours they watched in total last month. Here is a summary of the results:

Hours watched | Tablet | Smartphone
- | -: | -:
Mean | $6.5$ | $6.0$
Standard deviation | $2.6$ | $2.2$
Number of users | $67$ | $72$

The analyst wants to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{T}-\mu_\text{S}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{T}-\mu_\text{S} > 0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

Graphing calculators have the functionality to calculate both the test statistic and the P-value in a two-sample $t$ test. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

The degrees of freedom formula that calculators use to compute the P-value is pretty complex, but we get a more accurate P-value when we use technology to do tests like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $6.5$ | $6.0$
$s$ | $2.6$ | $2.2$
$n$| $67$ | $72$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 > \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} > 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 > \mu_2$
$t$ | $1.219$
P-value | $0.112$
df | $129.717$

The P-value is $\approx 0.112$, which is between $0.10$ and $0.20$.

$0.10 \leq P\text{-value}<0.20$

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher studying education in Italy and France wanted to compare how many years, on average, men in each country spend in school. The researcher obtained a random sample of men from each country. Here is a summary of the number of years the men in each sample spent in school:

Years in school | Italy | France
- | -: | -:
Mean | $10.8$ | $10.3$
Standard deviation | $2.3$ | $2.5$
Number of men | $46$ | $58$

The researcher wants to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{I}-\mu_\text{F}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{I}-\mu_\text{F} > 0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} > 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

- | -:

df | $99.72$

The P-value is $\approx 0.146$, which is between $0.10$ and $0.20$.

$0.10 \leq P\text{-value}<0.20$

Lifespan | Men | Women

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $13.2$ | $11.8$
$s$ | $2.8$ | $3.3$
$n$| $58$ | $62$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 > \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 > \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} > 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 > \mu_2$
$t$ | $2.511$
P-value | $0.007$
df | $116.914$

The P-value is $\approx 0.007$, which is less than $0.01$.

$P\text{-value}<0.01$

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A sociologist studying marriage in the United States and Canada wanted to compare how old, on average, women in each country are when they first get married. The sociologist obtained a random sample of married women from each country. Here is a summary of the ages at first marriage for the women in each sample:

Age at first marriage | US | Canada
- | -: | -:
Mean | $25.5$ | $26.4$
Standard deviation | $4.0$ | $3.5$
Number of women | $108$ | $102$

The researcher wants to use these results to test $\text{H}_0\text{: }\mu_\text{US}-\mu_\text{C}=0$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}\text{: }\mu_\text{US}-\mu_\text{C} < 0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**What is the P-value associated with these sample results?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy 

Graphing calculators have the functionality to calculate both the test statistic and the P-value in a two-sample $t$ test. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

The degrees of freedom formula that calculators use to compute the P-value is pretty complex, but we get a more accurate P-value when we use technology to do tests like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator:

|| **2-sample t-test** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $25.5$ | $26.4$
$s$ | $4.0$ | $3.5$
$n$| $108$ | $102$

* Alternative hypothesis: $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: }\mu_1 < \mu_2$
* Pooled: No

Note that our alternative hypothesis is $\text{H}_{\text{a}}\text{: } \mu_1 < \mu_2$, which is an equivalent way of writing $\mu_\text{1}-\mu_\text{2} < 0$.

Say "No" for "Pooled" in tests like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t test
- | -: 
Alternative hypothesis | $\mu_1 < \mu_2$
$t$ | $-1.738$
P-value | $0.042$
df | $206.813$

The P-value is $\approx 0.042$, which is between $0.01$ and $0.05$.

$0.01 \leq P\text{-value}<0.05$

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]
